key_departures.impact: The Bengals' best defensive player for five seasons and four-time Pro Bowler. Led NFL in sacks in 2024 (17.5). Was limited to 7 games in 2025 due to injury amid a yearslong contract dispute. Departs to a divisional rival (Ravens), where he will face Bengals twice per year. Massive hole at EDGE — he was the entire identity of CIN's pass rush.

Ossai had 5 sacks, 43 tackles, 12 QB hits, and 2 forced fumbles in 14 games in 2025. Was emerging as a reliable starter-level EDGE with back-to-back 5-sack seasons. Losing him compounded the Hendrickson departure and left the Bengals with almost no proven EDGE talent.

Stone started every game for four straight seasons and posted a career-high 104 tackles in 2025 with 13 interceptions over the last three seasons. Bengals signing Bryan Cook effectively replaced him in the starting lineup.
Bengals declined to tender Browning as a restricted free agent. With Joe Burrow's injury history (limited to 8 games in 2025), losing a known backup creates a need for a reliable QB2.
Former 2nd-round pick who started for three seasons in Cincinnati. His departure, combined with Marco Wilson also leaving, thins an already-struggling CB room.
Rotational CB depth departs alongside Taylor-Britt. Further weakens a cornerback group that was already below average.

Starting-caliber guard who departed. However, Bengals have Dalton Risner (re-signed) and Ted Karras at center, so the interior line is partially covered. Volson departure is a depth concern but not catastrophic.
Starting TE departs, though Tanner Hudson was retained on a one-year extension. Fant's departure creates a noticeable void at the top of the depth chart at tight end.
Veteran backup departs along with Browning. Leaves the QB room thin behind Burrow.
Depth EDGE rusher who contributed in a rotational role. Departure further depletes a thin edge room.
Depth/backup IOL. Minimal impact on the starting lineup.
Rotational LB departs. Bengals remain thin at linebacker with only rookie Barrett Carter and Demetrius Knight Jr. as the primary starters.
Depth safety departs. Bryan Cook signing replaces Geno Stone at the starter level, so this is a depth loss only.

signings.analysis: Three-year deal at $20M/year is solidly in the starter tier for EDGE. Mafe is fresh off a Super Bowl with Seattle and was eighth in pass rush win rate among all edge rushers in 2025 per Next Gen Stats despite recording only 2 official sacks. PFF credited him with 44 pressures, which would have led the Bengals last season. The guarantee structure ($19M) is modest relative to the total value, suggesting only the first year is truly locked in. This is a legitimate starter-level investment — not elite market, but real commitment. Mafe replaces one of the two departed starters (Ossai-level talent, not Hendrickson-level). EDGE remains a need given the double departure of Hendrickson and Ossai, but Mafe + Myles Murphy + Shemar Stewart gives a functional rotation. Need drops from tier 1 critical to tier 2.
signings.notes: Spotrac confirms 3yr/$60M with $19M guaranteed (roster bonus in Year 1). Cap hit of $17M in 2026 due to front-loaded structure.

signings.analysis: Three-year, $40.25M deal with $14M guaranteed (signing bonus that vests early in Week 1). Cook is a two-time Super Bowl champion with KC and posted 85 tackles and 6 passes defensed in 2025. The deal is front-loaded with $18M in Year 1 cash, signaling real commitment. Cincinnati native who attended UC — immediate cultural fit. He replaces Geno Stone alongside Jordan Battle and materially upgrades a safety room that was one of the team's biggest weaknesses. AAV of $13.4M is fair market for an ascending 26-year-old starting safety. Safety need fully addressed for the duration of the deal.
signings.notes: Contract confirmed via OTC and Spotrac. $14M fully guaranteed as signing bonus.

signings.analysis: Two-year deal (up to $28M with incentives) at $13M/year. Allen is a two-time Pro Bowler who posted 3.5 sacks, 68 tackles, and 34 pressures in 17 games for Minnesota in 2025. Vikings released him as a cap casualty. At 31, he is declining from his peak but posted the third-most pressures among all DTs on the Bengals roster if he'd been there last year. The effective guarantee is partially offset by $8M already owed by Minnesota, so Cincinnati's true cash outlay is lower. This is a short-term veteran fix — not a foundational signing. The 2-year term signals a bridge, not a long-term solution. IDL is partially addressed; Bengals likely still want a younger DT via the draft. Bengals retain B.J. Hill who is locked in with a $2M roster bonus.
signings.notes: Base deal confirmed at $26M/2yr via ESPN/Spotrac; max $28M with incentives per Tom Pelissero. $8M of Allen's contract offset by Vikings guaranteed salary remaining.

signings.analysis: Two-year extension through 2028 at $16M/year — essentially a paycut from his prior $16.023M/year but with restructured guarantees ($23M in first 12 months per beat reporter Kelsey Conway). Brown was entering the final year of his 4yr/$64M deal and could have tested the market. Instead, he negotiated the extension himself and stays as the starting LT. This marks the first time since the Joe Burrow era that CIN returns all five OL starters from the prior season. OT is no longer a need. Brown's declining PFF grades (60th-66th among tackles in recent years) mean the team may still draft a long-term LT eventually, but it is not a pressing need through 2028.
signings.notes: Extension terms reported by Kelsey Conway (Bengals beat) at $32M/2yr, $23M in Year 1 cash. Final guarantee structure pending official disclosure.

signings.analysis: One-year, $3.25M deal (up to $5M with incentives). Risner was a massive bargain — his 98.0% pass block efficiency was eighth-best among all starting guards in 2025. He is the returning right guard and slots directly into the starting lineup. However, the one-year structure signals this is not a long-term solution at IOL. With Cordell Volson gone, the depth behind Risner at guard is thin. IOL need is partially addressed: Risner returns as a solid starter but the depth and long-term picture at guard remain concerns.
signings.notes: Contract confirmed by official Bengals announcement March 2, 2026. Spotrac: 1yr/$3.25M base, $1M guaranteed, up to $5M with incentives.

signings.analysis: One-year extension for a depth/developmental CB. Does not address the departure of starters Cam Taylor-Britt and Marco Wilson. CB remains a significant need.
signings.notes: Official Bengals announcement Feb 20. Contract financial terms not disclosed.

signings.analysis: One-year extension for a backup TE. With Noah Fant departed, Hudson is now the top returning TE on the roster but has never been a true starter-level player. TE remains a real need.
signings.notes: Official Bengals announcement Jan 19. Contract terms not disclosed.

signings.analysis: Depth RB re-sign behind Chase Brown. No impact on team needs.
Special teams/depth WR re-sign. No impact on positional needs.
Depth WR extension. No meaningful need impact with Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins as the top two.
Special teams/depth safety extension. Bryan Cook signing already addressed the starting S need.
Special teams/depth LB extension. Does not address the starter-level LB need.
Special teams/depth LB extension. Does not address LB need.
Camp/depth TE extension. Does not address the Noah Fant departure at the top of the TE depth chart.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Bengals lost both Trey Hendrickson (Ravens, 4yr/$112M) and Joseph Ossai (Jets, 3yr/$36M) — their entire starting EDGE rotation. They countered with Boye Mafe (3yr/$60M, $19M guaranteed), a Super Bowl champion with elite pass rush win rate but only 2 official sacks in 2025. Mafe + Myles Murphy + Shemar Stewart is a functional rotation but lacks a proven sack producer. The Mafe signing partially addresses the need — it's a real 3-year investment at starter money — but it does not replicate what was lost. EDGE drops from catastrophic tier 1 to a real tier 2 need. Bengals should target an EDGE in rounds 2–3 of the draft.

Jonathan Allen (2yr/$26M, up to $28M incentives) gives Cincinnati a two-time Pro Bowl DT to pair with B.J. Hill. Allen posted 34 pressures in 2025, third-most of any DT who would have been on CIN's roster. However, Allen is 31, declining, was a cap casualty, and posted a career-low 53.2 PFF grade. The deal is a 2-year bridge, not a franchise solution. IDL drops from a tier 2 need to tier 3 — real veteran presence added, but a developmental IDL in the mid-rounds of the draft remains wise.

Bryan Cook signing (3yr/$40.25M, $14M guaranteed) fully addresses the safety need. Cook is 26, posted 85 tackles and 6 PDs in 2025, won two Super Bowls with KC, and brings legitimate starting caliber alongside Jordan Battle. Three-year commitment at real money. Safety is no longer a meaningful draft need.

Orlando Brown Jr. extension (2yr/$32M through 2028) eliminates the OT need. Bengals return all five OL starters for the first time in the Burrow era. OT is not a draft priority. The team may eventually want a developmental successor given Brown's declining PFF grades, but there is no urgency.

Dalton Risner re-sign (1yr/$3.25M) keeps the starting right guard in place and maintains OL continuity. However, Cordell Volson departed and depth behind Risner is thin. Ted Karras remains at center. IOL drops from tier 2 to tier 3 — starter is covered but depth needs a solution. A Day 3 IOL pick makes sense.

No LB was signed in free agency. The Bengals watched every meaningful LB option (Kaden Elliss, Leo Chenal) sign elsewhere. Brian Asamoah departed. Barrett Carter and Demetrius Knight Jr. — both 2025 rookies who struggled — are the primary starters. No veteran presence added. LB escalates to tier 1 — the Bengals must address this via the draft or late free agency or face a critical weakness on the second level.

Cam Taylor-Britt and Marco Wilson both departed. The only notable CB retained was Jalen Davis on a one-year depth deal. No outside CB was signed. The cornerback room is now dangerously thin. CB escalates to tier 1 — this needs to be addressed in rounds 1–3 of the draft.

Noah Fant departed (UFA). Tanner Hudson retained on a 1-year depth deal. No starter-level TE was signed. Hudson has never been a featured TE. TE need escalates from tier 3 to tier 2 — CIN should target a TE in rounds 3–5 of the draft.

Both Jake Browning (not tendered, going to Buccaneers) and Joe Flacco (UFA) departed. Burrow missed 9 games in 2025 with injury. The Bengals have no proven QB2 on the roster. Backup QB escalates to a real need — they must add a capable veteran backup before or during the draft.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion edge rusher brings top-8 pass-rush win rate to replace depleted CIN EDGE room
Two-time Super Bowl champion returns home to Cincinnati, immediately upgrades a safety room that ranked among the NFL's worst
Two-time Pro Bowl DT signs as cap casualty, adds veteran presence and 34 pressures to interior defensive line
Starting LT self-negotiates extension, locking in entire 2025 OL starting five for first time in Burrow era
Beloved right guard returns on team-friendly deal after posting 8th-best pass block efficiency among starting guards in 2025

key_losses.headline: Four-time Pro Bowler and 2024 NFL sacks leader departs to division rival Ravens after yearslong contract dispute — the most impactful departure in recent CIN history
Rising 25-year-old EDGE with back-to-back 5-sack seasons departs, compounding the Hendrickson loss and stripping CIN's pass rush
Four-year starting safety (104 tackles, 13 INTs over last 3 years) departs after Bengals prioritize Bryan Cook signing
Starting CB departs along with Marco Wilson, leaving the cornerback room critically thin heading into the draft
Starting TE departs with no replacement signed, creating a new need at a position that was previously stable
Bengals decline to tender RFA Browning, leaving Joe Burrow — who missed 9 games in 2025 — with no proven backup on the roster

notes: All major signings confirmed via official Bengals.com announcements, ESPN, NFL.com, OTC, and Spotrac. Jonathan Allen contract base confirmed at 2yr/$26M; max value $28M with incentives per Jeremy Fowler/ESPN and Tom Pelissero/NFL Network. Orlando Brown Jr. extension financial terms ($32M/2yr, $23M Year 1 cash) per beat reporter Kelsey Conway; official financials not formally disclosed by team as of publish date. Jonathan Allen guaranteed figure reflects $8M base salary guarantee; additional new-money guarantees from Bengals TBD pending full contract disclosure. Cap space post-FA reflects OTC data prior to Allen and Brown extension cap adjustments being fully processed — estimate ~$15-20M remaining effective cap room heading into the draft per historical CIN spending patterns.
